claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 3 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 2 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 3 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children I'm asking about - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. They're all young, under five years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie - all under five years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them, I need to ask a few questions:

First, do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

Next question: Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) currently live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't all live with me. Blake lives with me at home, but Alex and Charlie don't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask about each child separately.

For Alex and Charlie who don't live with you - are you paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them? This could include things like food, clothes, or pocket money.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, for Alex, I do help out with things - I contribute towards their clothes, food, and other things they need. For Charlie though, their other parent takes care of all that - they live with them and handle everything.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand a bit more about your contributions for Alex.

For Alex, do your contributions amount to at least the same as Child Benefit payments would be? For reference, Child Benefit is £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, and £16.95 per week for additional children. Are your contributions for Alex at least around that amount on a regular basis (weekly, monthly, or in lump sums)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute regularly for Alex - it works out to about £26 a week, so just over that threshold.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you, that's helpful. Now I need to ask about whether anyone else is currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these children.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, or Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: For Charlie, yes - the person they live with is already claiming Child Benefit for them. But for Alex and Blake, no one else is claiming.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me just confirm a few final details:

1. Do you have the right to reside in the UK? For example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another immigration status that gives you the right to reside here?

2. Are any of the three children currently in hospital, care, or residential accommodation for extended periods?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I'm a UK citizen, so I have the right to reside here.

And no, none of the children are in hospital, care, or residential accommodation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just one last question to complete my assessment:

Are any of your three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) being fostered by you, or are they being looked after under any arrangement where the local council is paying towards their accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are fostered, and the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

Thank you for providing all the information I needed. Here's my assessment for each of your children:

### **Blake - ELIGIBLE ✓**
You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake. Since Blake lives with you, is under 16 years old, you're a UK citizen living in the UK, and no one else is claiming for Blake, you meet all the requirements.

### **Alex - ELIGIBLE ✓**
You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you're making regular financial contributions of approximately £26 per week towards their upkeep (for clothes, food, and other needs). This exceeds the Child Benefit threshold, which means you're considered responsible for Alex. Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you qualify.

### **Charlie - NOT ELIGIBLE ✗**
You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie. Someone else (the person Charlie lives with) is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Under UK rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and you're not contributing towards Charlie's upkeep.

---

**Next Steps:** You can claim Child Benefit for Blake and Alex. If you haven't already done so, you'll need to make a claim through HMRC. 

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, so you may want to look into that as well.

reasonings: Alex does not live with you, but you are making regular financial contributions towards their upkeep of approximately £26 per week, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold of £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child or £16.95 for additional children. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child, which includes paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (e.g., on food, clothes, or pocket money). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. You live in the UK, are a UK citizen with the right to reside, and Alex is under 16. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake lives with you and is under 16 years old. You live in the UK and are a UK citizen with the right to reside. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and Blake is not in hospital, care, or residential accommodation. The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie does not live with you, and you are not contributing towards their upkeep - their other parent handles everything. Additionally, someone else (the person Charlie lives with) is already claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since someone else is already claiming for Charlie, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK citizen living in the UK with three children under five: Alex, Blake, and Charlie. Blake lives with the claimant, making them eligible. Alex doesn't live with the claimant, but the claimant contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which meets the threshold for responsibility under Child Benefit rules, and no one else is claiming for Alex, making them eligible. Charlie lives with someone else who is already claiming Child Benefit for them, and the claimant makes no contributions towards Charlie's upkeep. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, the claimant is not eligible for Charlie.

Additionally, someone else (the person Charlie lives with) is already claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since someone else is already claiming for Charlie, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK citizen living in the UK with three children under five: Alex, Blake, and Charlie. Blake lives with the claimant, making them eligible. Alex doesn't live with the claimant, but the claimant contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which meets the threshold for responsibility under Child Benefit rules, and no one else is claiming for Alex, making them eligible. Charlie lives with someone else who is already claiming Child Benefit for them, and the claimant makes no contributions towards Charlie's upkeep. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, the claimant is not eligible for Charlie.
